Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study in bursts, usually when I felt like I absolutely had to. With only about 10 hours a week, I'd spread it out randomly—maybe an hour here, two hours there. I learn better by doing things with my hands, so sitting and reading never felt natural to me. I'd often end up pacing around my room or fidgeting while trying to absorb material.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. If something was due, I'd cram. Otherwise, I'd drift through the week without touching course stuff. Life stuff kept interfering too—work, family obligations, just feeling mentally drained. Regular schedules sound nice in theory, but I could never stick to one.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was coming up, or I'd get a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing anything important. The anxiety would build up if I stayed away too long, so I'd force myself to at least look. It wasn't really enthusiasm driving me—more like fear of falling behind.
Q4: For a while, yeah, I got into a rhythm of checking in pretty regularly. I think it happened when I started participating in discussions more. Seeing other people respond to my posts made me want to come back. But it was fragile—one bad week emotionally and the whole routine fell apart. I had to rebuild it a few times throughout the course.
Q5: The discussion boards were actually helpful because I could engage with other students and it felt more active than just reading. Videos were decent too since I could pause and replay parts. Anything that let me interact or move at my own pace worked better for my learning style. Static PDFs and long readings? Those just made my eyes glaze over.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional resources. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment grade, I probably skipped it. Part of it was time—I didn't have internet at home, so I had to prioritize what I accessed when I could get online. The stuff that seemed extra just fell to the bottom of my list.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to my grade or to understanding something I was struggling with, it felt worth it. Also, if it was interactive or hands-on in some way. Busy work that felt like it existed just to fill time? That frustrated me. I needed to feel like my effort was actually going somewhere concrete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required stuff had urgency attached to it—I had to do it or face consequences. Optional things, even if interesting, got pushed aside because my energy was limited. When you're dealing with high anxiety and stress like I am, you have to pick your battles carefully.
Q9: Absolutely. Without internet at home, I'd download everything I could when I had access—PDFs, lecture slides, whatever. Then I'd study from those offline, sometimes for hours. I'd write out notes by hand, work through problems on paper, even build little models or diagrams to understand concepts. None of that showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be working through downloaded materials at home, making flashcards, practicing problems. The LMS would show nothing because I wasn't connected. I'd also talk through concepts out loud or teach them to my younger cousin to test my understanding. That kind of active learning doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or a PDF once, then spend days working through it offline. My login might show one quick session, but the actual studying happened later, disconnected. It was just practical given my situation—I had to make the most of my limited online time to grab resources for later.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods. When my anxiety spiked, everything felt overwhelming and pointless. I'd go through stretches where just getting out of bed was hard, let alone engaging with coursework. The CBT sessions helped me recognize those patterns, but in the moment, it was brutal. Family stress didn't help either.
Q13: Hugely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing on my mind. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them longer because starting felt impossible. Fatigue made me log in and just stare at the screen without really processing anything. My competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away from studying.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Physically present, mentally checked out. The sadness I've been carrying makes it hard to focus even when I'm trying. It's like there's fog in my head.
Q15: I procrastinated on bigger assignments even knowing they mattered. The anxiety made starting feel terrifying—like if I began and failed, it would confirm my worst fears about myself. So I'd avoid and avoid until the deadline forced my hand. It's a terrible cycle, but understanding it through therapy has helped me see it more clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I'd drift. As due dates approached, my LMS activity would spike—I'd suddenly be logging in constantly, watching videos, posting in discussions. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I functioned. The pressure was the only thing that cut through my fog.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it gave me a temporary boost. Made me feel like maybe I wasn't as hopeless as I thought. But the effect faded quickly. Negative or critical feedback would send me spiraling for days, making it harder to engage with anything. I'm pretty sensitive to how my work is received.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed a level of self-direction I struggled with. Having clear deadlines helped, but the pacing between them felt like dead zones where I'd lose momentum. More frequent check-ins or smaller milestones might have kept me on track better. I needed external structure because my internal structure is shaky.
Q19: The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced interaction, which I needed. But the heavy reading load was hard for someone like me who learns kinesthetically. More hands-on activities or practical applications would have engaged me better. Also, not having reliable internet made timed or synchronous elements really stressful.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and keeps checking things obsessively without actually producing work. Or someone who's struggling to understand the material and keeps reviewing without feeling ready to submit. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels out of fear or confusion.
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. My exam scores weren't amazing, but I passed, and my assignment scores were pretty good. A lot of my real learning happened offline where it didn't show. Someone looking at my low attendance and sporadic logins might think I wasn't trying, but I was working in ways the system couldn't see.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss everything that happens offline, all the emotional context, whether you're actually absorbing anything. They can't tell if someone's staring at a screen in tears or genuinely engaged. They capture behavior, not experience.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's when the material matters to you and you're mentally present. For me, engagement is rare and precious because my mind fights against it so often. When it happens, it feels like breaking through clouds.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought just to show up some days. It can't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the sadness that made everything feel pointless. It doesn't know about my offline studying, my lack of internet at home, or how the CBT sessions helped me push through. The numbers tell such a small part of my story.
